I've used LangGraph for about 8 months on a project that processed customer support tickets. We had three agents — one for triage, one for retrieval over our docs, and one for drafting replies. The state machine made debugging much easier than the LangChain agent loops I tried first, since I could see exactly where it got stuck.

For multi-agent state, I lean on LangGraph's checkpointer with SQLite for local dev and Postgres for prod. The hardest part was deciding what goes in the shared state vs what stays local to each node — we ended up keeping conversation history shared but tool results scoped per-agent.

Honestly I haven't shipped MCP in production yet, only a weekend prototype with the filesystem server. I read the spec and built a small custom MCP server that exposed three internal APIs as tools. I'd want to learn more before claiming production readiness.

When an agent hallucinates a clause that's not in the contract, my first move is to check whether the retrieval actually returned that chunk — usually it didn't, the model just made it up. We added a verification step where a second LLM call cross-references each cited clause against the source, and rejects outputs where the citation can't be grounded.

I'd want to know more about the existing eval setup before answering — whether you're using LangSmith, Langfuse, or rolling your own. In my last role we used Langfuse self-hosted and tracked tokens per node, latency p95, and a small golden-set of 50 contract reviews scored by a paralegal. The agreement between LLM judge and human was around 78%.
